weather.tip: Mostly clear and 27.6°C this evening with just 2% rain risk, so no umbrella needed for your commute home — but after this week's 33.6°C day, the hottest July reading in 112 years, keep carrying water through the warm afternoons until the heat breaks.

around.headline: Class 7 student critical after alleged harassment by teachers at Bengaluru school
around.summary: A 12-year-old boy is battling for his life after attempting suicide, which he and his family link to punishment and harassment by teachers at his Bengaluru school; a probe is underway. Parents should know their school's anti-bullying and grievance-redressal contacts, and take a child's complaints about teacher treatment seriously enough to follow up in writing.
around.source: The Times of India / NDTV / Deccan Herald

around.headline: Masked thief walks into Bengaluru finance firm, escapes with 13 laptops worth ₹10 lakh
around.summary: An unidentified masked man entered a finance company's office in Bengaluru and got away with 13 laptops worth close to ₹10 lakh. Offices should treat anyone without a verified visitor pass as unauthorized, no matter how routine their reason for entry sounds — hold the door only after reception confirms them.

around.headline: Bengaluru Metro's ₹1,274-crore push to add platform screen doors at all stations
around.summary: BMRCL is rolling out platform screen doors across all Namma Metro stations in a ₹1,274-crore safety upgrade, aimed at preventing falls and unauthorized track access. Expect station-wise work over the coming months, so build in a little extra time if your line is mid-upgrade.

around.headline: Election Commission extends Karnataka's SIR enumeration phase by 10 days, to August 8
around.summary: The Special Intensive Revision of electoral rolls in Karnataka now runs till August 8 instead of the earlier deadline. If you haven't submitted your enumeration form to the Booth Level Officer yet, you have extra time — but don't leave it to the last week.

around.headline: Clean it or pay for it: BBMP/GBA gives vacant-plot owners a 30-day deadline
around.summary: Bengaluru's civic body has told owners of vacant plots to clear garbage and overgrowth within 30 days or face cleanup charges billed to them, ahead of the citywide 'Freedom from Waste' drive starting August 1. Owners of empty sites should get them cleared now rather than risk a civic-imposed bill.

around.headline: Lok Adalat traffic-challan concession recovers ₹55 crore for Karnataka
around.summary: A discount scheme for settling pending traffic challans through Lok Adalat has brought in ₹55 crore in recoveries statewide. If you're sitting on old unpaid challans, check whether a Lok Adalat settlement window is still open in your area — it's usually cheaper than paying the full penalty later.

around.headline: US lowers threatened tariff on Russian-oil buyers from 500% to 100%, easing pressure on India
around.summary: Washington has scaled back a threatened tariff on countries buying Russian oil from 500% to 100%, a relief for India and China as major buyers. A lighter tariff threat reduces the risk of a sudden shock to India's oil import costs, which would otherwise feed into fuel and transport prices at home.

events.name: Mahesh Kale's Abhangwari concert
events.note: A classical vocal concert of Marathi devotional abhangs by Mahesh Kale — a good pick for a classical-music evening.

events.name: Checkmate Bengaluru: Chess Festival at Bhartiya Mall
events.when: Week-long, check venue listing for exact dates
events.note: A week-long chess festival featuring Arena Grandmaster Praveen Sagar, with free-play zones and workshops — good for players of any level.

events.name: One Night In Toki-O
events.note: A Japanese-themed evening of food, drinks, music and art — a good pick for something different on a night out.

events.name: Sheraton Grand Bengaluru's Persian culinary showcase
events.when: Two weeks, check hotel listing for dates
events.note: A two-week Persian food festival at Sheraton Grand — worth a visit if you enjoy exploring regional cuisines.

street_smart.title: A masked man walks into your office reception saying he's the vendor here to service the laptops. Do you let him through?
street_smart.based_on: Real case reported today: a masked man walked into a Bengaluru finance firm and escaped with 13 laptops worth nearly ₹10 lakh (The Times of India).
street_smart.situation: You're the only one at your office's front desk. A man wearing a mask and carrying a toolbag says he's from the IT vendor, sent to service some laptops, and asks you to let him through the locked inner door since he 'forgot' his visitor badge. What do you do?

choices.text: Let him through since he has a toolbag and sounds like he knows the department he's headed to.
choices.feedback: Sounding credible costs nothing — the masked thief who got away with 13 laptops from a Bengaluru finance firm this week didn't need forged paperwork, just enough confidence to walk past an unverified door. A toolbag and a department name are easy to fake; a verified visitor pass isn't.

choices.text: Ask him to wait at reception, call the actual employee or manager he claims to be visiting, and only let him in once they confirm and issue a visitor pass.
choices.feedback: Correct. A quick call to the person he claims to be meeting takes under a minute and closes the exact gap this week's theft exploited — nobody verified the visitor before the inner door opened. No confirmation, no access, regardless of how routine the story sounds.

choices.text: Let him in but walk with him the whole time so he can't wander off unsupervised.
choices.feedback: Escorting helps, but you've still granted access to someone whose identity was never confirmed — and a distraction at the right moment (a phone call, a colleague stopping you to chat) is all it takes to lose track of him. Verify first, escort second.

choices.text: Refuse entirely and tell him to leave the building, no exceptions.
choices.feedback: Blanket refusal is safe but impractical if he genuinely is the scheduled vendor — you'll end up turning away legitimate visitors and creating friction for no reason. The fix isn't refusing everyone, it's verifying before anyone gets through the door.

takeaways: Never open a locked or access-controlled door for someone without a verified visitor pass, no matter how routine or urgent their reason sounds.
A one-minute phone call to the employee or department a visitor claims to be meeting is the cheapest check that would have stopped this week's ₹10-lakh laptop theft.
Report masked or unusually evasive visitors to building security immediately, even if they leave when questioned — it helps flag repeat attempts.

quiz.q: A masked man this week escaped from a Bengaluru finance firm's office with how many laptops?
quiz.why: The laptops were worth close to ₹10 lakh combined — a reminder that unverified visitors, not just weak locks, are often the real security gap in an office.

quiz.q: Karnataka's Election Commission has extended the Special Intensive Revision (SIR) enumeration phase for electoral rolls to which date?
quiz.why: SIR enumeration forms are submitted to the local Booth Level Officer (BLO); missing the deadline can mean falling off the electoral roll entirely.

quiz.q: Namma Metro's ₹1,274-crore safety upgrade is installing what at all stations?
quiz.why: Platform screen doors are glass barriers that only open when a train has docked, and are widely used in metro systems to prevent falls and unauthorized track access.

quiz.q: The US recently lowered a threatened tariff on countries buying Russian oil from 500% to what figure?
quiz.why: India and China are among the largest buyers of Russian oil, so a lighter tariff threat reduces the risk of a sudden jump in India's oil import costs.

quiz.q: A 'Lok Adalat' in India is best described as what?
options: A people's court for amicable settlement of disputes, including traffic challans
A civic body that issues property tax notices
A consumer helpline for online complaints
quiz.why: Lok Adalats settle cases (including pending traffic fines) through negotiated agreement rather than a full trial, often at a discount to the original penalty — and the resulting order can't be appealed.
